Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause with second-person subject and stative verb construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: You are being overly suspicious or imagining threats that are not there.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in traditional woodland life where seeing a snake implied vigilance; metaphorically extended to paranoia.
First Example Sentence: Hash shilup ish pisa hosh, lawa nittak okla tok.
Second Example Sentence: Chi shilup ish pisa hosh ma, ikbi pisa la chike.
Third Example Sentence: Shilup ish pisa hosh kiyo, lawa achukma iksa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause with locative noun phrase and intensifying particle.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To exaggerate beyond reason; to make something bigger than it is.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agricultural metaphor referencing land measurement and expansion.
First Example Sentence: Chi nittak moma hosh anoli, lawa isht aiimpa tok.
Second Example Sentence: Nittak moma hosh anoli ma, pisa lawa tok.
Third Example Sentence: Hattak nittak moma hosh anoli la chike.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal predicate structure with metaphorical object substitution.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be blind to the obvious truth.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: You are ignoring what is obvious.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional metaphor comparing poor judgment to impaired sight.
First Example Sentence: Chi issi takkon hosh pisa ma, lawa pisa tok.
Second Example Sentence: Issi takkon hosh pisa kiyo, anumpa achukma chi.
Third Example Sentence: Ilap issi takkon hosh pisa tok.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound noun phrase with descriptive modifier functioning idiomatically.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Holisso (writing) historically sacred; misuse implied betrayal of trust.
First Example Sentence: Chi shilombish holisso aiimpa ma, miko hohchifo tok.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common emotional interjection in southeastern dialect communities.
First Example Sentence: Hashi lawa ia, ofi illi pisa tok.
Second Example Sentence: Hashi lawa ia ma, nan tvshka ikbi tuk.
Third Example Sentence: Sa anumpa tuk, hashi lawa ia.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause with instrumental noun and metaphorical extension.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Nan tvshka (work tools) metaphorically applied to speech craft.
First Example Sentence: Chi nan tvshka iksa hosh anoli ma.
Second Example Sentence: Nan tvshka iksa hosh anoli kiyo.
Third Example Sentence: Ilap nan tvshka iksa hosh anoli tuk.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Ofi (dog) behavior used metaphorically for cunning conduct.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal metaphorical construction with adjective intensifier.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To see clearly and understand wisely.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Vision associated with wisdom in traditional teachings.
First Example Sentence: Chi issi achukma hosh pisa ma.
Third Example Sentence: Ilap issi achukma hosh pisa li.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause with abstract noun intensifier.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To speak with great wisdom or authority.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Holisso (writing/knowledge) tied to leadership discourse traditions.
First Example Sentence: Miko holisso moma hosh anoli tok.
Second Example Sentence: Chi holisso moma hosh anoli ma.
Third Example Sentence: Hattak holisso moma hosh anoli li.
